facts.text: Yung Wing was born on November 17, 1828.
Yung Wing was born in the village of Nam Ping, about four miles southwest of Macao.
Nam Ping was situated on Pedro Island, west of Macao.
Yung Wing was one of four children in his family.
Yung Wing had an older brother, a sister, and a younger brother.
Yung Wing is the only survivor of his four siblings.
Mrs. Gutzlaff, wife of missionary Rev. Charles Gutzlaff, came to Macao in 1834.
Mrs. Gutzlaff started a girls' school for Chinese girls in Macao.
Mrs. Gutzlaff later opened a school for boys.
Yung Wing's parents chose to place him in Mrs. Gutzlaff's foreign school rather than a Confucian school.
Yung Wing's older brother was placed in a Confucian school.
Yung Wing's parents believed foreign intercourse with China would grow significantly.
In 1835, when Yung Wing was seven years old, his father took him to Macao to attend Mrs. Gutzlaff's school.
Yung Wing was initially frightened by Mrs. Gutzlaff's appearance and foreign dress.
Mrs. Gutzlaff eventually won Yung Wing over through kindness and sympathy.
Yung Wing came to regard Mrs. Gutzlaff as a mother figure.
Mrs. Gutzlaff kept Yung Wing among her girl pupils and did not allow him to mingle with boys.
Yung Wing attempted to run away from Mrs. Gutzlaff's school during his first year.
Yung Wing planned to escape by boat with six older girls from the school.
Yung Wing and the six girls were captured and brought back during their escape attempt.
Yung Wing was publicly punished by being placed on a table wearing a tall foolscap and placard labeled 'Head of the Runaways'.
Mrs. Gutzlaff distributed ginger snaps and oranges to other pupils as part of the punishment.
Mrs. Gutzlaff's school started in September 1835.
Mrs. Gutzlaff's school was originally for girls only.
A boys' department was temporarily incorporated into Mrs. Gutzlaff's school between 1835 and 1839.
Yung Wing was one of only two boys first admitted to Mrs. Gutzlaff's school.
The Misses Parkes, nieces of Mrs. Gutzlaff, came to China as assistants in the school.
The Misses Parkes were sisters to Harry Parkes, who was later knighted.
Harry Parkes played a conspicuous part in the second Opium War in 1864.
Mrs. Gutzlaff went to the United States with three blind girls named Laura, Lucy, and Jessie.
Yung Wing taught the three blind girls to read raised letters from the Bible and Pilgrim's Progress.
Yung Wing's father died in the fall of 1840 during the Opium War.
Yung Wing's father left four children and no means of support.
Yung Wing's brother was engaged in fishing.
Yung Wing's sister helped with housework.
Yung Wing took to hawking candy through his village and neighboring villages.
Yung Wing rose at three o'clock every morning to hawk candy.
Yung Wing did not return home until six o'clock in the evening while hawking candy.
Yung Wing's daily earnings from hawking candy were twenty-five cents.
Yung Wing turned over his earnings to his mother.
Yung Wing was engaged in hawking candy for about five months.
Yung Wing went into the rice fields to glean rice after the reapers.
Yung Wing's sister usually accompanied him during gleaning.
Yung Wing was about twelve years old when he gleaned rice.
Yung Wing's knowledge of English helped him earn extra rice sheaves from the head reaper.
Yung Wing recited the alphabet to the reapers and gleaners in the rice paddy.
A neighbor who was a printer in a Roman Catholic priest's office spoke to Yung Wing's mother about a job opportunity.
Yung Wing was hired to fold papers in a Roman Catholic priest's printing office in Macao.
Yung Wing's salary at the printing office was $4.50 a month.
Yung Wing's board and lodging cost $1.50 a month.
Yung Wing sent $3.00 a month to his mother from his printing office salary.
Yung Wing worked at the printing office for four months before being called away.
Dr. Benjamin Hobson was a medical missionary in Macao.
Dr. Hobson's hospital was about a mile from the printer's office.
Yung Wing knew Dr. Hobson from seeing him at Mrs. Gutzlaff's school.
Dr. Hobson had been searching for Yung Wing for months.
Mrs. Gutzlaff's last message to Dr. Hobson was to find Yung Wing and place him in the Morrison Education Society School.
The Morrison Education Society School was already opened for pupils when Dr. Hobson found Yung Wing.
Dr. Hobson wanted Yung Wing to stay with him at the hospital before introducing him to the Morrison School.
Yung Wing knew Dr. Hobson well from seeing him multiple times at Mrs. Gutzlaff's.
Mrs. Gutzlaff's last message to Dr. Hobson before embarking for America was to find Yung Wing and put him into the Morrison Education Society School.
Yung Wing's mother gave her consent for him to attend the Morrison School after some reluctance.
Yung Wing worked in a hospital with Dr. Hobson for about two months, preparing materials for ointments and pills and accompanying him on patient visits.
The Morrison School was opened on November 1, 1839, under the charge of Rev. S. R. Brown.
Rev. S. R. Brown and his wife landed at Macao on February 19, 1839.
Rev. S. R. Brown was a Yale graduate of the class of 1832 and was later made a D.D.
Yung Wing entered the Morrison School in 1841.
Five boys had entered the Morrison School one year ahead of Yung Wing: Wong Shing, Li Kan, Chow Wan, Tong Chik, and Wong Foon.
Yung Wing was the youngest of the first class of six boys at the Morrison School.
Yung Wing had an advantage over the other boys in reading and pronouncing English well.
The Morrison School studied English in the forenoon and Chinese in the afternoon.
Rev. S. R. Brown left China in December 1846 due to poor health.
Half of the original six students accompanied Brown to America on his return.
Dr. Robert Morrison died on August 1, 1834.
A circular calling for the formation of the Morrison Education Society was issued on January 26, 1835.
Dr. Robert Morrison was sent by the London Missionary Society as the first missionary to China in 1807.
Dr. Robert Morrison crossed the Atlantic from London to New York and embarked for China on January 31, 1807, in the sailing vessel Trident.
Dr. Robert Morrison was prevented from landing in Macao by the jealousy of the Jesuits.
Dr. Robert Morrison eventually made Malacca the basis of his labors.
Dr. Robert Morrison authored the first Anglo-Chinese dictionary of three quarto volumes.
Dr. Robert Morrison translated the Bible into Chinese.
Leang Afah was Dr. Robert Morrison's first Chinese convert and was trained by him to preach.
Leang Afah became a powerful preacher.
The Morrison School was removed to Hong Kong in 1842 after the close of the Opium War in 1840 and the ceding of Hong Kong to the British government.
The Morrison School was built on top of a hill about six hundred feet above sea level on the eastern end of Victoria Colony.
The hill where the Morrison School was built was called Morrison Hill.
William Allen Macy arrived in Hong Kong on March 12, 1845, as an assistant teacher in the Morrison School.
When Macy arrived, the Morrison School had been enlarged with three more classes of new pupils, totaling more than forty students.
Rev. S. R. Brown continued his work in the school until fall 1846.
Rev. S. R. Brown showed evidence of being a self-made man with a cool temperament and versatile adaptation of means to ends.
Rev. S. R. Brown was gentlemanly, agreeable, and somewhat optimistic.
Rev. S. R. Brown had an innate faculty for making things clear to pupils and conveying understanding without circumlocution.
Rev. S. R. Brown had experience as a pedagogue before coming to China and before entering college.
Rev. S. R. Brown proved himself to be a successful schoolmaster in his subsequent career in Auburn, New York, and in Japan.
William Allen Macy was a Yale man.
William Allen Macy had never taught school before coming to China.
William Allen Macy was well brought up with a sensitive nature and fine moral sensibilities.
William Allen Macy was full of earnestness and lofty ideals.
After the Morrison School was broken up in 1850, William Allen Macy returned to America with his mother.
William Allen Macy took up theology at Yale Theological Seminary.
In 1854, William Allen Macy went back to China as a missionary under the American Board.
Yung Wing had graduated from Yale College and was returning to China with Macy in 1854.
Yung Wing and Macy were the only passengers on a 154-day passage from Sandy Hook to Hong Kong.
Rev. S. R. Brown announced his contemplated return to America four months before he left China in winter 1846.
Rev. S. R. Brown offered to take some of his old pupils home with him to finish their education in the United States.
Wong Shing, Wong Foon, and Yung Wing decided to accompany Rev. S. R. Brown to America.
Yung Wing was the first to rise when Rev. S. R. Brown asked who wished to accompany him to the States.
Wong Foon was the second to rise, followed by Wong Shing.
Yung Wing's mother gave her consent for him to go to America with great reluctance and tears.
Yung Wing consoled his mother by noting she had two more sons and a daughter, and that his elder brother was engaged to be married.
The three Chinese boys were poor and could not have come to America without financial support.
Rev. S. R. Brown influenced the provision of support for the parents of the three boys for at least two years during their absence in America.
Andrew Shortrede, proprietor and editor of the Hong Kong China Mail, was a Scotsman and an old bachelor who helped defray the boys' expenses.
A. A. Ritchie was an American merchant who helped defray the boys' expenses.
A. A. Campbell was a Scotsman who helped defray the boys' expenses.
The Olyphant Sons—David, Talbot, and Robert—were three brothers and leading merchants of New York.
The Olyphant Brothers gave the three boys a free passage from Hong Kong to New York in their sailing vessel, the Huntress.
The Huntress brought a cargo of tea to New York.
Yung Wing, Wong Shing, and Wong Foon embarked at Whompoa on January 4, 1847, in the Huntress under Captain Gillespie.
The Huntress was loaded with a full cargo of tea.
The northeast trade wind blew strong and steady from Whompoa to St. Helena.
A gale occurred as the Huntress doubled the Cape of Good Hope.
During the gale at the Cape, the tops of the masts and ends of the yards were tipped with balls of electricity.
Yung Wing realized no danger during the gale but enjoyed the wild and weird scene.
The Huntress stopped at the Island of St. Helena for fresh water and provisions.
St. Helena appeared as a barren volcanic rock with no blade of grass on its burnt and charred surface.
The Huntress landed at Jamestown, a small village in the valley of St. Helena Island.
Yung Wing found a few Chinese inhabitants at St. Helena who were brought there by East India Company ships.
Yung Wing and his companions visited Longwood where Napoleon's empty tomb was located.
A large weeping willow hung and swept over Napoleon's tomb.
Yung Wing cut twigs from the weeping willow and kept them alive until reaching America.
Rev. S. R. Brown brought the willow twigs to Auburn, New York, and planted them near his residence.
The willows Yung Wing brought from St. Helena proved to be fine, handsome trees when he visited Auburn in 1854.
The Huntress took a northwesterly course and struck the Gulf Stream.
The Huntress landed in New York on April 12, 1847, after a passage of ninety-eight days of unprecedented fair weather.
New York in 1847 had only 250,000 or 300,000 inhabitants.
In 1845, while in the Morrison School, Yung Wing wrote a composition on 'An Imaginary Voyage to New York and up the Hudson.'
Yung Wing realized that his imagination sometimes foreshadows what lies uppermost in his mind and brings possibilities within the sphere of realities.
The Chinese Education Scheme came out of Yung Wing's day dreams a year before he graduated.
Yung Wing's marrying an American wife was another realization of his day dreams.
Prof. David E. Bartlett and his wife were the first friends Yung Wing made in the new world.
Prof. David E. Bartlett was a professor in the New York Asylum for the Deaf and Dumb.
Prof. David E. Bartlett was afterwards connected with a similar institution in Hartford.
Prof. David E. Bartlett died in 1879.
Mrs. Fanny P. Bartlett survived her husband for nearly thirty years and passed away in spring 1907.
Mrs. Fanny P. Bartlett was highly respected and beloved for her high Christian character and unceasing activities for good.
Mrs. Fanny P. Bartlett's influence extended to China through students who enjoyed her care and instruction.
Yung Wing counts Mrs. Fanny P. Bartlett as one of his most valued friends in America.
From New York, the three boys proceeded by boat to New Haven.
In New Haven, the three boys had an opportunity to see Yale College and were introduced to President Day.
Yung Wing did not then have the remotest idea of becoming a graduate of Yale College.
The three boys went by rail from New Haven to Warehouse Point and then to East Windsor.
East Windsor was the home of Mrs. Elizabeth Brown, wife of Dr. Brown.
Mrs. Elizabeth Brown's father was Rev. Shubael Bartlett, pastor of the East Windsor Congregational Church.
On their first Sabbath at East Windsor Congregational Church, the three Chinese boys sat in the pastor's pew on the left of the pulpit.
The three Chinese boys were the cynosure of the whole church during their first Sabbath.
Rev. Shubael Bartlett was a genuine type of the old New England Puritan.
Rev. Shubael Bartlett was exact and precise in all his manners and ways.
Rev. Shubael Bartlett spoke in a deliberate and solemn tone full of sincerity and earnestness.
Rev. Shubael Bartlett conducted himself as though treading on thin ice, cautiously and circumspectly.
Yung Wing became a graduate of one of the finest colleges in the country.
Yung Wing traveled by rail from New Haven to Warehouse Point and then to East Windsor.
Yung Wing stayed at the home of Mrs. Elizabeth Brown, wife of Dr. Brown, in East Windsor.
Three Chinese boys sat in the pastor's pew during their first Sabbath at the church.
Rev. Shubael Bartlett was an exact and precise man in all his manners and ways.
Rev. Shubael Bartlett never cracked jokes or burst out in open laughter.
Rev. Shubael Bartlett had his family Bible and hymn book placed in the same spot on the table every morning.
Rev. Shubael Bartlett always sat in the same spot for morning prayers.
Mrs. Bartlett was always cheerful with a smile lighting up her features.
Mrs. Bartlett had a kind and cheerful word for everyone.
Mrs. Bartlett's genial temperament and hospitality made the parsonage a favorite resort.
Rev. Shubael Bartlett's salary was not over $400 a year.
Daniel was the youngest son of Rev. Shubael Bartlett and worked hard to maintain the parsonage.
Yung Wing found a temporary home in the parsonage while at school in Monson and Yale.
Yung Wing went to Monson, Massachusetts to enter Monson Academy.
Monson Academy was a noted preparatory school in New England before high schools existed.
Rev. Charles Hammond was principal of Monson Academy and a graduate of Yale.
Rev. Charles Hammond was enthusiastically fond of the classics and a great admirer of English literature.
Rev. Charles Hammond was well-known in New England as an educator and champion of temperance.
Rev. Charles Hammond took special interest in the three Chinese students.
In their first year at the Academy, the Chinese students studied Greenleaf's Arithmetic, English Grammar, Physiology, and Upham's Mental Philosophy.
Miss Rebekah Brown was a graduate of Mt. Holyoke and valedictorian of her class.
Miss Rebekah Brown later became the wife of Doctor A. S. McClean of Springfield, Massachusetts.
Miss Rebekah Brown was a fine teacher and a woman of exceptional Christian virtues.
Doctor A. S. McClean and his wife gave Yung Wing a home during some of his college vacations.
Yung Wing made Springfield his headquarters in 1872 when he brought the first installment of Government students to the country.
Yung Wing and his companions boarded with Mrs. Phoebe H. Brown.
Yung Wing and his companions had a separate room in a dwelling across the road from Mrs. Brown's cottage.
Board and lodging at Monson cost only $1.25 a week per person.
Yung Wing and his companions had to take care of their own rooms and saw and split their own wood in winter.
Yung Wing's lodging was about half a mile from the academy.
Yung Wing walked to school three times a day in winter when snow was three feet deep.
Mrs. Phoebe H. Brown was a woman of surpassing strength of moral and religious character.
Mrs. Phoebe H. Brown authored the hymn 'I love to steal awhile away from every cumbering care.'
Rev. Charles Hammond was an enthusiastic admirer of Shakespeare.
Rev. Charles Hammond was partial to Daniel Webster among orators.
Rev. Charles Hammond introduced Yung Wing to Addison, Goldsmith, Dickens, Sir Walter Scott, the Edinburgh Reviews, Macaulay and Shakespeare.
During his first year at Monson Academy, Yung Wing had no idea of taking a collegiate course.
Yung Wing was expected to return to China at the end of 1849.
Wong Shing, the eldest of the three Chinese students, returned to China in fall 1848 due to poor health.
Yung Wing and Wong Foon decided to stay in the country to continue their studies.
Patrons in Hong Kong offered to support Wong Foon through a professional course at the University of Edinburgh.
Wong Foon decided to accept the offer and go to Scotland at the end of 1849.
Yung Wing preferred to remain in the country to continue his studies with a view to going to Yale.
Yung Wing and Wong Foon began their classical course in fall 1849.
Yung Wing and Wong Foon graduated from Monson Academy in summer 1850.
Yung Wing and Wong Foon first met as schoolmates at Morrison School in Macao in 1840.
Wong Foon attended the University of Edinburgh for seven years.
Wong Foon completed his professional studies as a doctor and returned to China in 1857.
Wong Foon graduated third in his medical class.
Wong Foon became one of the ablest surgeons east of the Cape of Good Hope.
Wong Foon had a fine practice in Canton where foreign residents retained him as their physician.
Wong Foon died in 1879.
Both native and foreign communities felt Wong Foon's loss.
Wong Foon was highly respected for his Christian character and purity of life.
Before entering Yale, Yung Wing had not solved the problem of financing his collegiate course.
Yung Wing appealed to Brown and Hammond for advice on financing his education.
Yung Wing was advised to avail himself of a contingent fund for indigent students.
The contingent fund required recipients to sign a pledge to study for the ministry and become a missionary.
Yung Wing decided it would be useless to apply for the fund due to the pledge requirement.
Yung Wing met with the trustees in the parsonage to discuss the fund.
The trustees offered to provide the fund if Yung Wing would pledge to return to China as a missionary.
Yung Wing refused to sign the pledge because it would handicap his freedom of action.
Yung Wing believed the calling of a missionary is not the only sphere where one can do good in China.
Yung Wing believed a pledge would prevent him from taking advantage of circumstances to serve China.
Brown and Hammond agreed that Yung Wing took the right view and sustained him in his position.
Brown visited the South in summer 1850 to see his sister.
Brown mentioned Yung Wing's case to members of 'The Ladies' Association' in Savannah, Georgia.
The Ladies' Association agreed to help Yung Wing in college.
Yung Wing passed his entrance examination for Yale.
Yung Wing had only fifteen months of Latin, twelve months of Greek, and ten months of mathematics before college.
Yung Wing's academy preparation was interrupted because the academy was broken up by the Palmer & New London Railroad.
Yung Wing passed his entrance examination without condition.
Yung Wing studied until twelve o'clock every night during his Freshman year.
Yung Wing took little or no exercise during his Freshman year and his health began to fail.
Yung Wing asked for a week's leave of absence during his Freshman year.
Yung Wing went to East Windsor to rest and returned refreshed.
Yung Wing had an utter aversion to mathematics, especially differential and integral calculus.
Yung Wing frequently failed mathematics during his Sophomore year.
Yung Wing thought he would be dropped from his class or dismissed from college due to his mathematics performance.
Yung Wing squeezed through his second year with a very low mark.
Yung Wing was afraid to ask his division tutor Tutor Blodget about his marks.
Yung Wing was a successful competitor in English composition in his division.
Yung Wing won the first prize in English composition in the second term of the year.
Yung Wing won the first prize in English composition in the third term of the year.
Yung Wing's English composition prizes gave him éclat in college and the outside world.
Yung Wing was not elated over his prizes due to his poor scholarship.
Before the close of his second year, Yung Wing secured the stewardship of a boarding club.
The boarding club consisted of sophomores and juniors with twenty members total.
Yung Wing did all the marketing and served at the table for the boarding club.
Yung Wing earned his board through the latter half of his college course via the boarding club stewardship.
Yung Wing received remittances from The Ladies' Association in Savannah.
Yung Wing received contributions from the Olyphant Brothers of New York.
Yung Wing was paid for being an assistant librarian to the 'Brothers in Unity' debating society.
Yung Wing assured his mother that as long as he lived all her comforts and wants would be scrupulously and sedulously looked after.
Yung Wing's mother told him to remove his mustaches because his older brother had not grown his yet.
Yung Wing promptly obeyed his mother's mandate to remove his mustaches.
Yung Wing's mother was satisfied that despite his foreign education, he had not lost his early training of being obedient to her.
Yung Wing had tender love for his mother.
Yung Wing's mother passed away in 1858 at the age of sixty-four.
Yung Wing's father died twenty-four years before his mother.
Yung Wing was in Shanghai at the time of his mother's death.
Yung Wing returned to his native village in time to attend his mother's funeral.
In the summer of 1855, Yung Wing took up residence in Canton with Rev. Mr. Vrooman, a missionary under the American Board.
Rev. Mr. Vrooman's headquarters were in Ham Ha Lan, near the government execution ground in the southwestern outskirts of Canton.
While in Canton, Yung Wing began his Chinese studies and commenced to regain the Canton dialect.
In less than six months, the Canton dialect came back to Yung Wing readily.
Yung Wing had studied the written Chinese language for only four years before leaving China in 1846.
Yung Wing was not well-grounded in the written language before leaving China in 1846.
The Chinese written language is stilted and full of conventional forms.
The Chinese written language is understood throughout the whole empire but differently pronounced in different provinces and localities.
In certain Chinese provinces like Fuhkien, Anhui and Kiangsu, the people are as foreigners to each other in the matter of dialects.
During the six months of Yung Wing's residence in Canton, Kwang Tung province was thrown into a disorganized condition.
In the summer of 1855, Viceroy Yeh Ming Hsin decapitated seventy-five thousand people in Canton.
Most of the seventy-five thousand people decapitated by Yeh Ming Hsin were innocent.
Yung Wing's residence in Canton was within half a mile of the execution ground.
Yung Wing ventured to walk to the execution ground out of curiosity.
The execution ground was perfectly drenched with human blood.
Headless human trunks were piled up in heaps on both sides of the driveway at the execution ground.
No provision had been made to facilitate the removal of the bodies from the execution ground.
The temperature in Canton in midsummer 1855 stood from morning to night steadily at 90° Fahrenheit and sometimes higher.
The atmosphere within a radius of two thousand yards of the execution ground was heavily charged with poisonous and pestilential vapor.
The bodies at the execution ground were left exposed for at least two days and showed signs of rapid decomposition.
A deep and extensive ravine in the far-off outskirts of the western part of Canton was converted into a sepulchral receptacle for the corpses.
Countless swarms of reddish-hued worms covered the corpses in the ravine without earth being thrown over them.
During June, July and August of 1855, seventy-five thousand people had been decapitated in Canton.
More than half of the seventy-five thousand people decapitated were declared to be innocent of the charge of rebellion.
The accusation of rebellion was made as a pretext to exact money from the victims.
Yeh Ming Hsin was appointed viceroy of Kwang Tung and Kwangsi in 1854.
Yeh Ming Hsin was a native of Han-Yang.
Han-Yang is a part of the port of Hankau.
Han-Yang was destroyed when the Taiping rebels took possession of it.
Yeh Ming Hsin had immense estates in Han-Yang which were completely destroyed by fire.
The destruction of his estates embittered Yeh Ming Hsin towards the Taiping rebels.
The Taiping leaders hailed from Kwang Tung and Kwangsi.
Yeh Ming Hsin transferred his hatred of the Taiping rebels to the people of Kwang Tung and Kwangsi.
Yeh Ming Hsin did not deign to give the victims even the semblance of a trial.
Several years after 1855, Yeh Ming Hsin got into trouble with the British government.
Yeh Ming Hsin was captured by British forces and banished to an obscure and remote corner in India.
Yeh Ming Hsin led an ignominious life in India, hated by the whole Chinese nation and despised by the world at large.
After visiting the execution ground, Yung Wing felt faint-hearted and depressed in spirit.
After visiting the execution ground, Yung Wing had no appetite for food.
After visiting the execution ground, Yung Wing was too nervous for sleep at night.
Yung Wing thought the Taiping rebels had ample grounds to justify their attempt to overthrow the Manchu régime.
Yung Wing's sympathies were thoroughly enlisted in favor of the Taiping rebels.
Yung Wing thought seriously of making preparations to join the Taiping rebels.
Upon calmer reflection, Yung Wing fell back on the original plan of recovering the Chinese language and following the logical course of things.
Yung Wing succeeded in mastering the spoken language sufficiently to speak it quite fluently.
Yung Wing set to work to find a position in which he could support himself and his mother.
Yung Wing wanted to form a plan for working out his ideas of reform in China.
Doctor Peter Parker had been a medical missionary under the American Board for many years in Canton.
Doctor Peter Parker was made United States Commissioner as a temporary expedient.
Dr. Parker was given the appointment of commissioner on account of his long residence in China and his ability to speak Chinese.
Dr. Parker was not given the appointment on account of any special training as a diplomat or legal knowledge.
M. N. Hitchcock was an American merchant of the firm of Messrs. King & Co.
M. N. Hitchcock was a mutual friend of Dr. Parker and Yung Wing.
Through M. N. Hitchcock, Yung Wing became Dr. Parker's private secretary.
Yung Wing knew Dr. Parker while he was at Mrs. Gutzlaff's School.
Dr. Parker knew Yung Wing had recently graduated from Yale.
Yale was Dr. Parker's Alma Mater.
Dr. Parker's headquarters were in Canton, but he spent his summers in Macao.
Yung Wing was with Dr. Parker for only three months.
Yung Wing's salary as Dr. Parker's secretary was $15 a month.
Dr. Parker had very little for Yung Wing to do.
Yung Wing thought that by being identified with Dr. Parker, he might possibly come in contact with Chinese officials.
Yung Wing could neither learn anything from Dr. Parker nor enlarge his acquaintance with Chinese officials.
Yung Wing gave up his position as Dr. Parker's secretary and went to Hong Kong to try to study law.
Andrew Shortrede was Yung Wing's old friend.
Andrew Shortrede generously extended hospitality of his house to Yung Wing.
Through Andrew Shortrede, Yung Wing secured the position of interpretership in the Hong Kong Supreme Court.
The interpretership in the Hong Kong Supreme Court paid Yung Wing $75 a month.
Yung Wing was advised to apprentice himself to an attorney or solicitor-at-law.
In English court practice, there are two distinct classes of lawyers: attorneys or solicitors, and barristers.
Attorneys prepare in writing all evidences, facts, and proofs of a case and hand them to the barrister or counsel.
Barristers argue the case in court according to law.
Yung Wing apprenticed himself to an attorney recommended by Andrew Shortrede.
Yung Wing was not aware that by going into Hong Kong to become an attorney, he was stepping on the toes of the British legal fraternity.
All the attorneys in Hong Kong banded themselves together against Yung Wing.
The attorneys stated in all the local papers except the 'China Mail' that if Yung Wing were allowed to practice, they might as well pack up and go back to England.
The attorneys feared Yung Wing would monopolize all the Chinese legal business because he had complete knowledge of both English and Chinese.
The attorneys made it too hot for Yung Wing to continue in his studies.
The attorney-general of Hong Kong wanted Yung Wing to apprentice himself to him.
The attorney-general did all he could to use his influence to open the way for Yung Wing to become an attorney.
The attorney-general drafted a special colonial ordinance to admit Chinese to practice in Hong Kong Colony.
The colonial ordinance was sent to the British government to be sanctioned by Parliament.
The colonial ordinance was sanctioned and became a colonial law.
Anstey was the attorney-general of Hong Kong.
Anstey found out that Yung Wing had apprenticed himself to Parson, the attorney.
After Anstey found out about Yung Wing's apprenticeship to Parson, Yung Wing had no peace.
Yung Wing was between two fires: the batteries operated by the attorneys and the battery operated by the attorney-general.
The attorney-general found fault with Yung Wing's interpreting, which he had never done previously.
Mr. Parson was under hot fire from both sides.
Mr. Parson told Yung Wing plainly and candidly that he had to give him up.
Mr. Parson made the article of apprenticeship between them null and void.
Yung Wing had to give up his position as interpreter in the Supreme Court.
Mr. Parson gave up his business in Hong Kong and returned to England not long after Yung Wing abandoned his apprenticeship.
Master and pupil left their posts at pretty nearly the same time.
Yung Wing believed it was the best thing that he did not succeed in becoming a lawyer in Hong Kong.
The theatre of action in Hong Kong would have been too restricted and circumscribed for Yung Wing.
Yung Wing could not have come in touch with the leading minds of China had he been bound up in Hong Kong.
Yung Wing might have made a fortune if he had succeeded in his legal profession in Hong Kong.
Circumstances forced Yung Wing to leave Hong Kong.
Yung Wing's mind was directed northward to Shanghai.
In August 1856, Yung Wing left Hong Kong in the tea clipper 'Florence'.
Captain Dumaresque of Boston was the captain of the 'Florence'.
Captain Dumaresque was altogether a different type of man from the captain of the 'Eureka' which brought Yung Wing out in 1855.
Captain Dumaresque was kind, intelligent and gentlemanly.
When Captain Dumaresque found out who Yung Wing was, he offered him a free passage from Hong Kong to Shanghai.
Captain Dumaresque was the sole owner of the 'Florence'.
The 'Florence' was named after Captain Dumaresque's daughter, Florence.
The passage from Hong Kong to Shanghai lasted only seven days.
Before the passage was over, Yung Wing and Captain Dumaresque became great friends.
Not long after his arrival in Shanghai, Yung Wing found a situation in the Imperial Customs Translating Department.
Yung Wing's salary in the Imperial Customs Translating Department was Tls. 75 a month, equivalent to $100 Mexican.
The Mexican dollar was adopted for want of a Chinese silver currency.
The duties in the Imperial Customs Translating Department were not arduous and trying.
The duties in the Imperial Customs Translating Department were too simple and easy to suit Yung Wing's taste and ambition.
Yung Wing had plenty of time to read in his position in the Imperial Customs Translating Department.
Before three months of trial in his new situation, Yung Wing found that things were not as they should be.
Between the interpreters and the Chinese shippers there existed a regular system of graft.
Yung Wing did not wish to be implicated with the others in the division of the spoils.
Yung Wing made up his mind to resign from the Imperial Customs Translating Department.
Yung Wing called upon the Chief Commissioner of Customs to find out what his future prospects were.
Yung Wing was told that no prospects of promotion to the position of commissioner were held out to him or to any other Chinese interpreter.
Yung Wing decided to throw up his position in the Imperial Customs Translating Department.
Yung Wing sent in his resignation, which was at first not accepted.
Lay was the chief commissioner of customs.
Lay strenuously tried to persuade Yung Wing to change his mind.
Lay offered to raise Yung Wing's salary to Tls. 200 a month as an inducement.
Lay thought Yung Wing was only bluffing in order to get higher wages.
Yung Wing valued a clean reputation and an honest character more than money.
Yung Wing saw no reason why he should not be given the same chances to rise in the service of the Chinese government as an Englishman.
Yung Wing believed his individuality should be recognized and respected in every walk of life.
Yung Wing had aspirations even higher than Lay's.
Yung Wing did not care to associate himself with a pack of Custom-house interpreters and inspectors known to take bribes.
Yung Wing believed a man who expects others to respect him must first respect himself.
Yung Wing did not state the real cause of his quitting the service.
After four months' trial, Yung Wing left the Imperial Customs Translating Department service.
Yung Wing left the Imperial Customs service to try his fortune in new fields more congenial.
Yung Wing's friends looked upon him as a crank in throwing up a position yielding Tls. 200 a month for something uncertain and untried.
Yung Wing's friends thought throwing up his position was the height of folly.
Yung Wing had made three shifts in less than a year since his return to China.
Yung Wing began to think he was too mercurial to accomplish anything substantial.
Yung Wing began to think he was too dreamy to be practical or too proud to succeed in life.
Yung Wing believed that in a strenuous life one needs to be a dreamer in order to accomplish possibilities.
Yung Wing believed people are not called into being simply to drudge for an animal existence.
Yung Wing had to work hard for his education.
Yung Wing believed that a man who expects others to respect him must first respect himself.
Yung Wing left the Imperial Customs service after four months of trial.
Yung Wing quit a position yielding 200 Tls. per month to pursue something uncertain and untried.
Yung Wing's friends considered his decision to leave his position foolish.
Yung Wing had made three shifts in employment within less than a year of returning to China.
Yung Wing worried he was too mercurial to accomplish anything substantial.
Yung Wing believed that in a strenuous life one needs to be a dreamer to accomplish possibilities.
Yung Wing worked hard for his education.
Yung Wing wanted to make his education a blessing common to his race rather than just for individual benefit.
Yung Wing sought to find his true bearing and make himself a blessing to China.
Yung Wing worked as a clerk in an English tea and silk merchant house.
Yung Wing gained insight into mercantile business methods during his time with the English firm.
The English firm dissolved partnership after six months, throwing Yung Wing out of his position.
Yung Wing attended a prayer meeting at the Union Chapel in Shanghai on a Thursday evening.
Yung Wing calmly confronted drunken foreign sailors who had snatched his servant's lantern and attempted to kick him.
Yung Wing obtained the name and vessel information of the offending first mate from sober sailors.
The offending first mate was the first mate of the ship Eureka, which brought Yung Wing to China in 1855.
Yung Wing wrote a note to the captain of the Eureka to summon the first mate ashore.
Yung Wing advised the first mate that Americans in China were held in high esteem and should not compromise that reputation.
Yung Wing declined the first mate's invitation to go aboard ship for wine.
A Scottish man tied cotton balls to Yung Wing's queue at an auction sale.
Yung Wing asked the Scotchman to untie the cotton balls in a pleasant way.
The Scotchman responded with disdain and scorn to Yung Wing's request.
The Scotchman punched Yung Wing in the mouth without drawing blood.
Yung Wing struck the Scotchman back in the mouth with a blow that drew blood profusely.
The head partner of the firm separated Yung Wing and the Scotchman during their fight.
Yung Wing told the crowd he was only defending himself against insult and injury.
The British Consul remarked that Yung Wing was too fiery and lost the advantage of a legal suit by retaliating.
The Scotchman shut himself in his room for a week after the incident to allow his wound to heal.
The incident created a sensation in the foreign settlement and became a chief topic of conversation.
Chinese in Shanghai regarded Yung Wing with great respect for defending his rights against a foreigner.
No Chinese within the foreign settlement's jurisdiction had previously been known to defend their rights against a foreigner.
Yung Wing believed Chinese people had been meek and mild in allowing personal insults to pass unresented.
Yung Wing believed the triumph of Japan over Russia had opened the eyes of the Chinese world.
Yung Wing believed China would no longer tolerate injustice or foreign aggression.
Yung Wing's knowledge of the Chinese language had decidedly improved within two years.
Yung Wing took to translating as a means of bridging breaks in his employment.
Yung Wing's chief concern was to widen his acquaintance with educated and mercantile classes.
Yung Wing's translating business brought him in contact with the comprador of a leading Shanghai house.
A senior partner of the leading house died in 1857.
Yung Wing was selected as one of two translators to translate an epitaph for the deceased partner.
Yung Wing's translation was given preference and accepted by the firm's manager.
Yung Wing's translation was adopted and engraved on the monument.
Yung Wing became known among the Chinese as a Chinese student educated in America.
The Yellow River inundated the northern part of Kiangsu province, making thousands homeless and destitute.
A Chinese deputation asked Yung Wing to draw up a circular appealing to the foreign community for aid.
Yung Wing's circular resulted in subscriptions of no less than $20,000 within a week.
Yung Wing wrote a letter of acknowledgment and thanks to the foreign community on behalf of the committee.
Yung Wing's letter was published in the Shanghai Mail and Friend of China.
Within three months of starting his translating business, Yung Wing became widely known in Shanghai.
Yung Wing was indebted to Tsang Kee Foo, the comprador, for his translating business opportunities.
Tsang Kee Foo was a well-educated Chinese man highly respected for his probity and intelligence.
Tsang Kee Foo had gathered around him some of the finest Chinese scholars from all parts of China.
Tsang Kee Foo introduced Yung Wing to the celebrated Chinese mathematician Li Jen Shu.
Li Jen Shu later brought Yung Wing to the notice of Viceroy Tsang Kwoh Fan.
Viceroy Tsang Kwoh Fan promoted the Chinese Education Scheme.
Yung Wing believed it is almost impossible to know which friends may prove to be the key to one's destiny.
Tsang Kee Foo introduced Yung Wing to the manager of Messrs. Dent & Co.
The manager of Messrs. Dent & Co. offered Yung Wing a position as comprador in Nagasaki, Japan.
Yung Wing declined the comprador position.
Yung Wing believed the compradorship is associated with all that is menial.
Yung Wing felt that as a Yale graduate, accepting a comprador position would bring discredit to his Alma Mater.
Yung Wing entertained profound respect and reverence for Yale College.
Yung Wing was jealous of Yale's proud fame.
Yung Wing preferred to travel for the firm as its agent in the interior rather than serve as comprador.
Yung Wing would not sacrifice his manhood for the sake of making money in a servile position.
Yung Wing preferred to pack tea and buy silk as an agent on salary or commission.
Yung Wing was poor financially but refused to accept a menial position.
The manager Webb remarked that Yung Wing was poor but proud.
Webb decided to send Yung Wing to the tea districts to learn the business of packing tea.
On March 11, 1859, Yung Wing boarded a Woo-Sik-Kwei, a Chinese fast boat.
The Woo-Sik-Kwei was built in Woo-Sik, a city on the borders of the Grand Canal near Suchau.
Yung Wing decided to return to Shanghai in late August via Hankau and the Yangtze River rather than via Siang Tan.
Yung Wing arrived at Hankau on August 29th, having left there two months earlier in July.
Yung Wing arrived in Shanghai on the night of September 30th after a seven-month journey from March to October.
Yung Wing's first journey into the interior of China gave him insight into the actual condition of the people during a drastic rebellion.
Yung Wing was struck by the sparseness of population in the sections of Chêhkiang, Kiangsi, Hunan and Hupeh he visited.
An English friend requested Yung Wing to buy raw silk in Shau Hing after his arrival from the interior in October.
Yung Wing spent about two months in the silk business in Shau Hing.
Yung Wing was taken down with fever and ague in Shau Hing and was compelled to give up the business.
Yung Wing recovered from the fever and ague and left Shau Hing for fresher air.
In the fall of 1859, Yung Wing was asked to join a small party of two missionaries and Tsang Laisun to visit the Taiping rebels in Nanking.
Yung Wing decided to join the trip to find out the character of the Taipings and whether they were fitted to set up a new government.
Yung Wing left Shanghai on November 6th, 1859 in a Woo-Sik-Kwei boat.
Yung Wing's party had an American flag on board but hauled it down to avoid attracting undue attention.
Yung Wing's party avoided the Sung-Kiang route to avoid being hauled up by imperialists.
Yung Wing reached Suchau on the morning of November 9th without meeting difficulty or obstacles.
Yung Wing's party met Commandant Liu in Suchau, who gave them a letter of introduction and passports.
Yung Wing met four foreigners in Commandant Liu's audience hall: two Americans, one Englishman, and a French noble.
Yung Wing's party left Suchau on the morning of November 11th.
Yung Wing's party was courteously treated by rebels at Woo Sik and invited to dinner by the chief in command.
Yung Wing left Woo Sik on November 12th and started for Cheong Chow.
Yung Wing observed that the country surrounding the canal between Suchau and Tan Yang had been abandoned by peasantry.
Yung Wing reached Cheong Chow in the night and found nearly all houses along the road to be deserted.
Yung Wing resumed his journey to Tan Yang on November 13th at six o'clock in the morning.
Yung Wing came to anchor near Tan Yang for the night on November 13th.
Yung Wing went into Tan Yang early the next morning to see Commandant Liu but found him absent.
Yung Wing's party met with Chin, a civilian next to Liu, who treated them kindly and with great civility.
Yung Wing's party dined with Chin in the evening on November 14th.
Yung Wing's party started on foot on November 16th from Tan Yang to Nanking.
Yung Wing walked fifteen miles from Tan Yang to a village called Po Ying.
Yung Wing reached Ku Yung on November 17th at about nine o'clock in the morning.
Yung Wing traveled from Tan Yang to Nanking in November 1860.
Yung Wing walked fifteen miles from Tan Yang to a village called Po Ying on November 16.
Yung Wing reached Nanking on November 18 after a trying and wearisome journey.
Yung Wing had seen Rev. Mr. Roberts in Macao twenty or more years before when in Mrs. Gutzlaff's school.
Yung Wing made the acquaintance of Kan Wong in 1856 at Hong Kong.
Kan Wong was a nephew of Hung Siu Chune, the rebel chief styled Tien Wong.
Kan Wong had been connected with the London Mission Association as a native preacher under Dr. James Legge.
Yung Wing told Kan Wong he had no intention of casting his lot with the Taipings.

Yung Wing proposed seven measures to Kan Wong: organizing an army on scientific principles, establishing military and naval schools, organizing civil government, establishing a banking system, establishing an educational system, and organizing industrial schools.
Yung Wing said he would offer his services to help carry out the proposed measures if the Taiping government was willing.
Kan Wong had seen more of the outside world than the other princes or leaders.
Kan Wong understood the secret of the strength and power of the British government and European powers.
Kan Wong was alone and had no one to back him in advocating the proposed measures.
Yung Wing received a wooden seal from Kan Wong designating him with the title of 'E' meaning 'Righteousness,' the fourth official rank under that of a prince.
Yung Wing decided to return the seal and decline the rank.
Yung Wing asked Kan Wong for a passport guaranteeing safe conduct through Taiping territory.
Yung Wing received a passport on December 24.
Yung Wing started his return trip to Shanghai on December 27.
Yung Wing arrived safely in Tan Yang in early January 1861.
Yung Wing believed the Taiping Rebellion differed from previous Chinese rebellions by incorporating a religious element.
Yung Wing credited Dr. Robert Morrison and Rev. Icabod J. Roberts with imparting knowledge of Christianity to Hung Siu Chune.
Yung Wing believed the corruption of the administrative government was the foremost cause of the rebellion.
Yung Wing believed official bribery and exploitation were main causes of the rebellion.
Yung Wing believed the whole administrative government was founded on a system of fraud and falsehood.
Yung Wing had strong doubts about the final success of the Taiping cause based on the conduct and character of its leaders.

The Taiping movement had Christian tendencies including worship of God, Christ as Savior, the Holy Spirit, destruction of temples and idols, prohibition of opium, observance of Sabbath, and prayers before meals and battles.
The Western Christian world initially believed China would be evangelized through the Taiping movement and the Manchu Dynasty would be overthrown.
Hung Siu Chune named his desired new government the 'Celestial Empire of Universal Peace.'
The Taipings achieved initial victories on their route from Kwang Si to Nanking.
The Taipings sent a division of their army toward Peking that was defeated at Tientsin.
The Taipings recruited new forces from Hunan, Hupeh, Kiang Si, and part of An Hwui during their march.
The newly recruited Taiping forces were undisciplined and engaged in pillage, plunder, and indiscriminate destruction.
The Taiping cause lost prestige after their defeat before Tientsin.
The Taipings captured Yang Chow, Suchau, and Hangchau, which were wealthy cities known for beautiful women.
Wealth and luxury from captured cities accelerated the Taipings' decline.
The Taiping Rebellion lasted fifteen years before collapsing.
The Taiping Rebellion left no traces of Christian elements in Nanking or Kwang Si.
The Taiping Rebellion produced no new political ideas or theories for a new form of government.
Yung Wing's visit to Nanking was barren of hope for promoting educational or political reform.
Yung Wing decided to make a fortune as his first duty before pursuing other plans.
Yung Wing met tea merchants in a Shanghai tea garden who told him about green tea in the Taiping district of An Hwui province.
The tea merchants stated there were over 1,000,000 chests of tea in the Taiping district ready for shipment.
Yung Wing's friend Tsang Mew was a man of great business experience and had previously sent him traveling into the interior.
Tsang Mew consulted with the head of his firm about Yung Wing's tea project.
Yung Wing's company decided to fund his tea expedition project.
Yung Wing planned to determine the safest route for the tea expedition between Wuhu and Ta Tung.
Wuhu was a treaty port under rebel jurisdiction.
Ta Tung was not a treaty port and was in possession of the imperialists.
The distance from Wuhu to Taiping by river was about two hundred and fifty miles.
Yung Wing chose Wuhu as the basis for his preliminary expedition.
Yung Wing traveled with four Chinese tea-men who were natives of Taiping and refugees in Shanghai.
Three cities passed on the way to Taiping were mostly deserted but occupied by rebels.
Paddy fields along the river were left uncultivated and overrun with weeds and grass.
The Taiping district had a population of 500,000 before the conflict.
Only a few dozen people remained in the Taiping district, appearing emaciated and like walking skeletons.
Yung Wing reached the village of San Kow after a week's journey.
Three tea-men who had been in Shanghai four years previous met Yung Wing in San Kow.
Yung Wing selected the best intact house in San Kow as his headquarters for tea business.
San Kow contained at least five hundred thousand boxes of tea.
The whole Taiping district contained at least a million and a half boxes of tea, with about sixty pounds per box.
Yung Wing found the river route from Wuhu to Taiping to be perfectly safe.
Yung Wing sent samples of different kinds of green tea to Shanghai for inspection.
Yung Wing was appointed head of all succeeding expeditions to escort treasure and tea.
In one expedition, Yung Wing had a staff of six Europeans and six Chinese tea-men.
The expedition carried eight boxes of treasure containing 40,000 taels (about $53,000 in Mexican dollars).
The expedition had a fleet of eight tea-boats: four large and four smaller ones.
Yung Wing had about forty men total including boatmen for the expedition.
The six European escorts were principally runaway sailors picked up in Shanghai.
One European was an Englishman who professed to be a veterinary doctor and was over six feet tall.
The expedition left Wuhu in fine spirits.
The expedition stopped for the night in a secluded cove near King Yuen, which was about halfway to San Kow.
Yung Wing had obtained a passport in Nanking.
Yung Wing ordered all firearms to be examined, loaded, and distributed before retiring for the night.
Watchmen were stationed in each boat and paid extra for night watch duty.
Yung Wing felt nervous and could not sleep the night of the attack.
A marauding band of approximately one thousand men attacked the expedition from the opposite riverbank.
The river between the boats and the marauding band provided protection from immediate attack.
Pitch darkness concealed the boats from the attackers' sight.
The English veterinary doctor advocated passive surrender and showed signs of fear.
Yung Wing proposed to use his yellow silk passport to parley with the rebel chief rather than fight.
Yung Wing threatened to report any theft to authorities in Nanking to recover losses.
The marauding horde was embarking in boats and moving downstream.
The attack occurred at approximately three o'clock in the morning when it began to rain.
Advance boats from the marauding band passed the expedition without discovering their location.
Yung Wing threatened to report the marauding horde's actions in Nanking and recover losses.
Yung Wing and his men waited anxiously on the forward decks of boats for danger to pass.
The marauding horde was embarking in boats along the opposite shore and moving downstream.
It was three o'clock in the morning when it began to rain.
Yung Wing and his party owed their safety to darkness, rain, and their location in a retired cove.
Yung Wing reached San Kow two days later in peace and safety.
Yung Wing sent the first installment of fifteen boatloads of tea to Wuhu within two weeks.
The second installment consisted of twelve boatloads of tea.
Yung Wing escorted the second installment down the river in person.
The river was quite shallow in some places, especially in summer.
Yung Wing jumped into the water up to his waist to set an example for reluctant boatmen.
Yung Wing was engaged in the Taiping tea business for about six months.
Yung Wing took away about sixty-five thousand boxes of tea.
Yung Wing became ill with fever and ague of the worst type.
Yung Wing was laid up sick in Shanghai for nearly two months.
Yung Wing gave up the Taiping tea enterprise due to health concerns.
Yung Wing believed his life was of inestimable value and more important than fortune.
Yung Wing was well-known among foreign merchants and native business men.
Yung Wing was offered a tea agency in Kew Keang for packing teas.
Yung Wing gave up the tea agency in less than six months.
Yung Wing started a commission business on his own account.
Yung Wing continued his commission business for nearly three years.
Yung Wing had been weaving an educational scheme during the last year of his college life.
In 1863, Yung Wing was prospering in his business.
Yung Wing received an unexpected letter from Ngan Khing in 1863.
The letter was from Chang Shi Kwei, an old friend from Shanghai whom Yung Wing met in 1857.
Chang Shi Kwei was a native of Ningpo.
Chang Shi Kwei was in charge of the first Chinese gunboat owned by the local Shanghai guild.
Chang Shi Kwei had become one of Tsang Kwoh Fan's secretaries.
Tsang Kwoh Fan had heard of Yung Wing and wished to see him.
Yung Wing initially questioned whether Tsang Kwoh Fan wanted to trap him.
Yung Wing knew Chang Shi Kwei's character well and trusted him.
Yung Wing replied that he could not accept the invitation due to the tea season.
A second letter came two months after the first, urging Yung Wing to come to Ngan Khing.
Li Sien Lan enclosed a letter in the second correspondence.
Li Sien Lan was a distinguished Chinese mathematician.
Yung Wing had made Li Sien Lan's acquaintance while in Shanghai.
Li Sien Lan assisted Mr. Wiley in translating mathematical works into Chinese.
Yung Wing had failed mathematics in his sophomore year of college.
Yung Wing acknowledged that mathematics was left out of his make-up.
Li Sien Lan was also an astronomer.
Li Sien Lan told Tsang Kwoh Fan that Yung Wing had a foreign education.
Yung Wing had raised a handsome subscription to help famine refugees in 1857.
Yung Wing had a strong desire to help China become prosperous, powerful and strong.
Chu and Wa were interested in machinery and were invited to Ngan Khing by the Viceroy.
Li Sien Lan's letter dispelled Yung Wing's doubts about the viceroy's intentions.
Yung Wing wrote that in a couple of months he would be more at liberty to take the journey.
In July 1863, Yung Wing received a third letter from Chang and a second from Li.
Tsang Kwoh Fan wanted Yung Wing to give up his mercantile business and serve the state government.
Yung Wing saw the possibility of carrying out his educational scheme with Tsang Kwoh Fan's support.
Yung Wing replied that he would come to Ngan Khing after winding up his business within a month.
Yung Wing planned to start by August at the latest.
The correspondence marked the initiatory step of Yung Wing's official career.
Tsang Kwoh Fan was regarded by his contemporaries as a great scholar and learned man.
Tsang Kwoh Fan drilled an army of his own compatriots from Hunan.
Hunan men had a reputation for being brave and hardy fighters.
Tsang Kwoh Fan secured cooperation from other Hunan men to build a flotilla of river gun-boats.
The river gun-boats played a great and efficient part as an auxiliary force on the Yangtze River.
The gun-boats helped check the rapid concentration of rebel forces.
Lost provinces were gradually recovered over a few years.
The Taiping Rebellion was narrowed down to the single province of Kiang Su.
Nanking was the capital of the rebellion and the only stronghold left.
Nanking succumbed to Tsang Kwoh Fan's forces in 1864.
Tsang Kwoh Fan was made the generalissimo of the imperialists.
Tsang Kwoh Fan was invested with almost regal power.
The revenue of seven or eight provinces was placed at Tsang Kwoh Fan's disposal.
Official ranks and territorial appointments were at Tsang Kwoh Fan's command.
Tsang Kwoh Fan was literally and practically the supreme power of China at the time.
Tsang Kwoh Fan never abused the almost unlimited power placed in his hands.
Tsang Kwoh Fan did not enrich himself, his family, relatives or friends with vast resources.
Li Hung Chang was Tsang Kwoh Fan's protégé and successor.
Li Hung Chang bequeathed 40,000,000 Tls. to his descendants after his death.
Tsang Kwoh Fan died comparatively poor.
Tsang Kwoh Fan kept the escutcheon of his official career untarnished.
Tsang Kwoh Fan left a name and character honored for probity, patriotism and purity.
Tsang Kwoh Fan had great talents but was modest.
Tsang Kwoh Fan had a liberal mind but was conservative.
Tsang Kwoh Fan was a perfect gentleman and nobleman of the highest type.
Yung Wing came in contact with Tsang Kwoh Fan in the fall of 1863.
Yung Wing took passage in a native boat and landed at Ngan Khing in September.
Yung Wing was met by Chang Si Kwei, Li Sien Lan, Wha Yuh Ting and Chu Siuh Chune at Ngan Khing.
All of Yung Wing's greeters were old friends from Shanghai.
Tsang Kwoh Fan had manifested curiosity and interest in Yung Wing for the past six months.
Tsang Kwoh Fan's interest in Yung Wing stemmed from hearing that he had gone to America as a boy.
Yung Wing's Shanghai friends claimed credit for praising him to the viceroy.
Yung Wing's Shanghai friends knew what the viceroy wanted him for.
Yung Wing was ushered into Tsang Kwoh Fan's presence without waiting in the ante-room.
Tsang Kwoh Fan sat in silence smiling and scanning Yung Wing from head to foot.
Tsang Kwoh Fan's keen eyes seemed to attract special attention to Yung Wing's eyes.
Yung Wing felt uneasy but was not abashed during the meeting.
Tsang Kwoh Fan asked Yung Wing how long he had been abroad.
Yung Wing answered that he was absent from China eight years in pursuit of a Western education.
Tsang Kwoh Fan asked if Yung Wing would like to be a soldier in charge of a company.
Yung Wing said he would be pleased to head a company if he had been fitted for it.
Yung Wing stated he had never studied military science.
Tsang Kwoh Fan said Yung Wing would make a fine soldier based on his appearance.
Tsang Kwoh Fan said he could see from Yung Wing's eyes that he was brave and could command.
Yung Wing thanked the viceroy for the compliment.
Yung Wing acknowledged he may have the courage of a soldier but lacked military training and experience.
Tsang Kwoh Fan asked Yung Wing's age and whether he was married.
The first interview lasted only about half an hour.
Tsang Kwoh Fan began to sip tea, signaling the end of the interview according to Chinese official etiquette.
Yung Wing returned to his room and told his Shanghai friends what had passed.
Yung Wing's Shanghai friends were highly delighted by the interview.
Tsang Kwoh Fan was over sixty years of age in 1863.
Tsang Kwoh Fan was five feet, eight or nine inches tall.
Tsang Kwoh Fan was strongly built and well-knitted together.
Tsang Kwoh Fan had a broad chest and square shoulders.
Tsang Kwoh Fan had a large symmetrical head.
Tsang Kwoh Fan had a broad and high forehead.
Tsang Kwoh Fan's eyes were set on a straight line under triangular-shaped eyelids.
Tsang Kwoh Fan's eyes were free from obliquity characteristic of the Mongolian type.
Tsang Kwoh Fan had high cheek bones.
Tsang Kwoh Fan's face was straight and somewhat hairy.
Tsang Kwoh Fan allowed his side whiskers their full growth.
Tsang Kwoh Fan's full beard swept across his broad chest and added dignity.
The interview with the viceroy lasted only about half an hour.
Sipping tea according to Chinese official etiquette signals that an interview is ended.
Yung Wing's Shanghai friends were highly delighted when he told them about his meeting with the viceroy.
Tsang Kwoh Fan was strongly built and well-proportioned.
Tsang Kwoh Fan's eyes lacked the obliquity characteristic of the Mongolian type of countenance.
Tsang Kwoh Fan had a full beard that swept across his broad chest.
Tsang Kwoh Fan's eyes were keen and penetrating.
Tsang Kwoh Fan's eyes were of a clear hazel color.
Tsang Kwoh Fan had a large mouth that was well compressed with thin lips.
Tsang Kwoh Fan's thin lips showed a strong will and high purpose.
Tsang Kwoh Fan was one of the most remarkable men of his age and time.
Tsang Kwoh Fan rose from being a Hanlin scholar to become a generalissimo.
Tsang Kwoh Fan rose through indomitable persistence and perseverance.
Tsang Kwoh Fan headed Hunan raw recruits against the Taiping rebels.
Tsang Kwoh Fan reduced the Taiping Rebellion from covering three provinces to a single province.
Tsang Kwoh Fan succeeded in crushing the Taiping Rebellion by the fall and capture of Nanking.
The Taiping Rebellion lasted fifteen years, from 1850 to 1865.
The Taiping Rebellion cost the Empire untold treasures.
Twenty-five million human lives were lost in the Taiping Rebellion.
The Dowager Empress was grateful to Tsang Kwoh Fan for restoring peace to the Manchu Dynasty.
The Dowager Empress created Tsang Kwoh Fan a duke.
Tsang Kwoh Fan's greatness was measured by his virtues rather than his victories.
Tsang Kwoh Fan was known for his pure and unselfish patriotism.
Tsang Kwoh Fan was known for his deep and far-sighted statesmanship.
Tsang Kwoh Fan maintained the purity of his official career.
Tsang Kwoh Fan was known in history as 'the man of rectitude.'
Tsang Kwoh Fan received the posthumous title 'the man of rectitude' by imperial decree.
Yung Wing spent nearly two weeks in the viceroy's headquarters.
Yung Wing occupied a suite of rooms in the same building as his Shanghai friends Li, Chang, Wha, and Chu.
At least two hundred officials were living in Tsang Kwoh Fan's military headquarters.
Tsang Kwoh Fan had no less than a hundred secretaries.
Tsang Kwoh Fan's headquarters included expectant officials, learned scholars, lawyers, mathematicians, astronomers, and machinists.
Tsang Kwoh Fan had great admiration for men of distinguished learning and talents.
Tsang Kwoh Fan loved to associate and mingle with learned men.
Yung Wing's Shanghai friends had frequent interviews with the Viceroy about establishing a foreign machine shop in China.
Yung Wing's friends gave him a dinner where the machine shop subject was discussed.
Yung Wing believed a machine shop in China should be of a general and fundamental character rather than for specific purposes.
Yung Wing based his views on personal observation in the United States and common sense.
